narrative:
Let's take a moment to understand the prediction made by the model regarding your bar exam outcome. Currently, the model predicts that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, which led to the rejection of your university application. This prediction is based on various aspects of your academic performance and background compared to other students who have taken the exam.

To help you understand what could change this prediction, we use something called counterfactual scenarios. These are "what if" situations that show how altering certain factors could lead to a different outcome, specifically, a prediction that you would pass the exam.

In your case, the model identified several key areas that could influence the prediction. Notably, your academic deciles in Year 1 and Year 3, family income, LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, gender, racial background, and whether you work full-time or part-time were considered. Among these, the scenarios suggest that changes in your undergraduate GPA and your work status (from full-time to part-time) are significant. These features consistently appeared in scenarios where the prediction flipped to a pass.

Out of the scenarios generated, the most frequent changes involved your undergraduate GPA and work status. Specifically, a slight improvement in your GPA or switching to part-time work could significantly impact the prediction. Interestingly, your family income, LSAT score, and demographic factors like gender and race did not require changes in these scenarios, indicating they are less critical for altering the prediction.

The patterns suggest that focusing on improving your GPA and considering a shift to part-time work could be effective strategies. For instance, aiming to raise your GPA closer to the average of 3.21 or adjusting your work commitments could enhance your chances of passing. These changes are realistic and actionable, providing a clear path forward.

By understanding these insights, you can make informed decisions about where to focus your efforts. Remember, these are just model predictions and not definitive outcomes. With dedication and strategic adjustments, you can improve your chances of success.
